title: Safety and Lockout/Tagout Before You Touch Anything

questions.q: What does Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) require you to do before hands-on work?
options: Shut off every energy source, apply YOUR own lock and tag, then verify zero energy with your meter
Just flip the main switch off and start working
Hang a tag but leave the power on for testing
Have a coworker hold the switch off for you
questions.why: LOTO means de-energizing all sources, applying your own lock and tag so no one can re-energize it, and proving zero energy before you touch anything.

questions.q: Your control circuit is only 24VDC, which feels safe. Why must you STILL lock out the motor starter before working on it?
options: The starter's power side can still carry lethal voltage like 480VAC even when the 24VDC control is off
The 24VDC could climb to 480V on its own
LOTO is only paperwork and doesn't really matter here
The control voltage is the only dangerous part
questions.why: Turning off the 24VDC control does nothing to the separate power side, which may still carry lethal 480VAC 3-phase to the motor.

questions.q: After you lock and tag a disconnect, what is the final step before you trust the equipment is dead?
options: Verify zero energy by testing with your meter (live-dead-live)
Assume it's dead because the lock is on
Wait five minutes and start working
Ask the operator if it feels off
questions.why: A lock alone doesn't prove the circuit is dead; you confirm zero energy by metering it, using live-dead-live so you can trust your meter.

questions.q: You finish LOTO and verify-dead on the control side, but the print shows a separate 480V feed you haven't checked. What should you do?
options: Identify and lock out that source too — LOTO must cover ALL energy sources
Ignore it because you already locked one source
Work on it anyway since the control side is dead
Re-energize everything to test it first
questions.why: LOTO means de-energizing every source feeding the equipment, including stored energy; one unchecked 480V feed can still kill you.

questions.q: You trip the main breaker on a VFD-fed pump, wait 45 seconds, open the drive enclosure, and set your meter to DC volts across the bus terminals (P and N). It reads 480VDC. What do you conclude?
options: The meter is malfunctioning since the breaker is already open
480VDC is normal residual noise and safe to work around
The DC bus capacitors haven't bled down yet — close the cover and wait for the label's full bleed-down time before touching anything
The line terminals must also be live, so re-check the main breaker
questions.why: VFD DC bus capacitors hold a lethal charge for 3-5 minutes after shutoff regardless of what the line terminals or pilot light show; the manufacturer's bleed-down label tells you the real wait time. Assuming 45 seconds is enough, or that a live reading means the meter is wrong, is exactly how techs get shocked reaching into a drive.

questions.q: After opening a 120V circuit's disconnected wire, your standard 10-megohm meter reads 45VAC on it. You're about to tell the crew it's live and needs another lockout point. What should you check first?
options: Assume 45VAC confirms a second hot feed and lock out another source immediately
Switch to LoZ mode (or load the wire with a plug-in resistance) — a standard high-impedance meter commonly shows 40-80VAC ghost voltage on an open wire near a live neighbor conductor
Replace the meter's battery since it's giving a false-low reading
Ignore the reading since anything under 120VAC full-scale is always safe
questions.why: A 45VAC reading on an open conductor in a busy conduit is the classic signature of induced ghost voltage from capacitive coupling to an adjacent live wire, not a real hazard. LoZ mode or loading the circuit with a resistance collapses ghost voltage but leaves true voltage intact — that's how you tell the difference instead of chasing a phantom lockout point.

questions.q: You're the millwright on a blender that's also being worked by an electrician. Per OSHA 1910.147 group lockout, what is the correct way to lock it out?
options: Whoever arrives first locks it out, and the other person just signs the tag
One shared plant lock goes on the hasp and both of you keep a copy of the key
Only the electrician needs to lock it out since the electrical hazard is worse
Each of you applies your own personal lock and tag to the hasp, and the machine can't start until every lock is removed
questions.why: Group LOTO requires every person working on the equipment to hold their own lock on the hasp, with the key in their own pocket, so no one can be re-energized on while still inside. A shared lock or a single locker's lock covering multiple trades breaks the rule that you never work under someone else's lock.

questions.q: A control panel schematic shows one motor feeding this frame, dated 1991. Before you write the LOTO procedure for a repair, what should you do based on the war story where an unrecorded retrofit added a second 480V feed?
options: Trust the schematic since it's the official plant document
Ask the operator if any other power feeds were added recently
Lock out only the motor shown on the print and proceed with the repair
Physically trace every conductor entering the machine frame yourself before trusting the print, since retrofits often go unrecorded
questions.why: The war story's lesson was explicit: the print is history, the machine is truth. An unrecorded retrofit added a second 480V hydraulic pump feed that wasn't on the 1991 schematic, and a millwright who trusted the print lost two fingers. Verbal confirmation from an operator isn't a substitute for physically tracing conductors.

questions.q: During live-dead-live testing on a 480V 3-phase feeder after lockout, how many individual meter readings should you take to fully confirm zero energy?
options: One: just L1 to ground is enough since all three phases are tied together
Three: one reading per phase to ground only, skipping phase-to-phase
Two: L1-L2 and L1-ground cover every possible path
Six: L1-ground, L2-ground, L3-ground, L1-L2, L2-L3, and L1-L3
questions.why: Three conductors require checking every conductor-to-ground pair AND every conductor-to-conductor pair — six readings total — because a fault or backfeed could show up on any single path while the others read zero. Skipping phase-to-phase checks (like testing only to ground) can miss an energized path between two hot legs.

questions.q: A technician skips live-dead-live and just trusts a single 'zero' reading from their meter on a de-energized 480V conductor, not knowing the meter has a blown internal fuse. What is the real danger this creates?
options: The meter will simply refuse to turn on, so the tech would notice immediately
The meter can display zero on a fully live 480V circuit, giving false confidence that leads the technician to grab a lethal conductor believing it's dead
A blown fuse only affects current measurements, so voltage readings are unaffected in any meter
The worst outcome is an inaccurate reading that's off by a few volts, not a false zero
questions.why: A meter with a blown fuse, dead battery, or damaged probe can read zero on a live 480V circuit exactly as if it were dead — that false confidence is what live-dead-live is designed to catch, by proving the meter reads correctly on a known live source immediately before and after testing the de-energized point. Without that bracketing test, a bad meter and a live conductor look identical to a dead one.
